1	# Robyn — integration-point map (current state)
2	
3	Terse, citation-heavy. Paths relative to repo root `/Users/devansh/hackathonProjects/encode-xero-hack`. Read `CLAUDE.md` and `HANDOFF.md` before touching anything; this file only maps where things live.
4	
5	---
6	
7	## 1. Backend (api/, NestJS)
8	
9	### Module registration — `api/src/app.module.ts`
10	- Global `ConfigModule` loads `api/.env` (app.module.ts:24-27). TypeORM Postgres with `synchronize: true`, entities from `ALL_ENTITIES` (app.module.ts:28-35, entity barrel `api/src/entities/index.ts`).
11	- `ScheduleModule.forRoot()` at app.module.ts:36 (cron support).
12	- Shared kernel: `AuditModule`, `XeroModule`, `LlmModule`, `EngineModule` (app.module.ts:38-41). Feature surfaces: `ClientsModule, MeetingsModule, EmailModule, ProposalsModule, DetectionsModule, TasksModule, ConnectionsModule, DashboardModule, HealthModule` (app.module.ts:43-51). **A new feature module gets imported here** — keep the kernel/surfaces grouping.
13	
14	### Meetings module — `api/src/modules/meetings/`
15	- **Entities**: `Meeting` (`api/src/entities/meeting.entity.ts`): `gcalEventId` (unique, :14-16), `title/start/end`, `durationHours` (numeric, :28-29), `attendees` jsonb (:31-32), `clientId` nullable uuid (:34-36), `state` enum `MeetingState` default DETECTED (:38-40), `transcriptId`, `skipReason`, `matchProposals` jsonb, `isPersonal`, `source` `'google'|'ics'` (:56-57). `Transcript` (`api/src/entities/transcript.entity.ts`): `meetingId`, `rawText`, `source` enum (PASTED/GRANOLA/UPLOAD), `parsed` jsonb `TranscriptParsed` (:8-19).
16	- **Endpoints** (`meetings.controller.ts`): `GET /api/meetings` (:31-40, optional `?state=`), `GET /api/meetings/:id` (:42-51), `POST /api/meetings/sync` (:53-63 — Google if configured else `.ics` fixture, then Loop-1 on eligible meetings), `POST /api/meetings/import-ics` (:65-74, body `ImportIcsDto.icsText`), `POST /api/meetings/:id/transcript` (:76-87), `POST /api/meetings/:id/confirm-client` (:89-99), `POST /api/meetings/:id/skip` (:101-111). Cron: calendar sync every 15 min, gated on `isServing()` (:113-118; `api/src/common/bootstrap-flag.ts:6-8` — `ROBYN_SERVE=1` set only by `main.ts:10`, never during OpenAPI export).
17	- **How meetings get billing state**: `MeetingsService.runLoop1` (`meetings.service.ts:109-373`). Deterministic classify (engine, :125), LLM only proposes fuzzy matches for humans (:126-149, "The LLM never sets clientId"). Pre-proposal decision via `engine.step` (:166); state machine at `api/src/modules/engine/state-machine.ts:32-99` (states: personal→SKIPPED, UNKNOWN→UNKNOWN_ATTENDEE+queue potential client, no transcript→AWAITING_TRANSCRIPT+PROVIDE_TRANSCRIPT task, AMBIGUOUS→CONFIRM_CLIENT_MATCH task, MATCHED+transcript→build proposal; policy decides autoSend vs REVIEW_INVOICE). Proposal persisted idempotently by `reference = ROBYN-<meetingId8>` (:264-280). Auto-send path writes to Xero via `xero.writeInvoice` (:294-304) and flips `MeetingState.SENT`; failure or review path raises `REVIEW_INVOICE` task (:332-339, :356-363).
18	- Calendar ingest: `ingestCalendar` (:569-588) — Google when `cfg.google.configured` (dynamic `googleapis` import :674-724, needs `GOOGLE_ACCESS_TOKEN`/`GOOGLE_REFRESH_TOKEN`), else `.ics` fixture from `seed/data/calendar.ics` (path candidates :611-627). Upsert keyed on `gcalEventId` (:726-764). CALENDAR ConnectionState upserted at `upsertCalendarConnection` (:924-932).
19	- Task raise/resolve helpers with `dedupeKey = type:refId` live here (:776-843) — reuse this pattern; do not invent a second task path.
20	
21	### Connections module — `api/src/modules/connections/`
22	- **Entity** `ConnectionState` (`api/src/entities/connection-state.entity.ts`): unique `kind` (XERO/CALENDAR/EMAIL), `status` (LIVE/FALLBACK/DOWN), `label`, `detail`, `lastSyncAt`, `nextPollAt` (:9-26). Enums at `api/src/entities/enums.ts:84-94`.
23	- **LIVE/FALLBACK decision per provider**: XERO row is *probed live* every read via `xero.health()` — never persisted on GET, no FALLBACK state for Xero (LIVE or DOWN only) (`connections.service.ts:77-115`). CALENDAR row is upserted by MeetingsService (`meetings.service.ts:924-932`; LIVE when Google works :575-580, FALLBACK for `.ics` :601-608, DOWN when no file :592-599). EMAIL row is upserted by EmailService (`email.service.ts:281-298`; LIVE when `cfg.imap.configured` :69-71, DOWN if IMAP configured-but-unreachable :101, FALLBACK for fixture mailbox).
24	- **Endpoints** (`connections.controller.ts`): `GET /api/connections` (:12-21, stable order XERO/CALENDAR/EMAIL), `POST /api/connections/xero/recheck` (:23-33 — probes, upserts XERO row, audits, returns row; never throws on Xero down).
25	
26	### Email module — `api/src/modules/email/`
27	- One endpoint: `POST /api/email/poll` (`email.controller.ts:13-17`). **Poll flow** (`email.service.ts:58-234`): load PotentialClients in QUEUED/WATCHING (:63-67) → if `cfg.imap.configured` read IMAP per queued sender with `SINCE lastPolledAt` (:390-446), else read fixture mailbox `seed/data/mailbox/*.json` once (:79-82, :316-345) and filter to queued senders only (:114-116). **Potential-client filter is a hard invariant**: "never reads a non-queued sender — that scope widening is a bug" (:37-41). Each message classified by `llm.classifyAgreement` (:124); on agreement: PotentialClient → AGREEMENT_DETECTED with evidence (:157-167), CONFIRM_AGREEMENT task raised idempotently by dedupeKey (:237-278), audit on every edge. EMAIL ConnectionState upserted at :281-298; nextPollAt = +30 min (:35, :61).
28	
29	### Env config — `api/src/config/env.ts`
30	- `MissingEnvError` + `req()` assert-at-boot pattern (:5-19); `loadConfig()` cached (:50-86). Required: `DATABASE_URL`, `ANTHROPIC_API_KEY`, `XERO_CLIENT_ID/SECRET` (:64-68). Optional blocks: `xero.scopes` (`XERO_SCOPES` override) and `xero.paymentAccountCode` (:69-70); **imap block** `IMAP_HOST/PORT/USER/PASSWORD` with `configured` boolean (:72-78); **google block** `GOOGLE_CLIENT_ID/SECRET` with `configured` (:79-83). New env vars go here, following `req`/`opt` and the `configured` flag pattern; `main.ts:11` calls `loadConfig()` so a missing required var kills boot.
31	
32	### Audit service usage pattern — `api/src/modules/audit/audit.service.ts`
33	- `audit.record({ actor, action, summary, subjectType?, subjectId?, inputs? })` (:6-34). Actors: ROBYN/HUMAN/LLM/XERO/SYSTEM (`entities/enums.ts:98-104`). Action strings are dotted lowercase (`meeting.transcript_attached`, `xero.invoice.sent`, `task.raised`, `policy.decided`, `connection.xero.recheck`). Reads: `audit.list(limit)` (:36-38) and `audit.listForSubject(subjectId, limit)` (:40-46). Every state change, Xero write, policy decision, and poll goes through this — see e.g. meetings.service.ts:133-147, :254-261, :317-325; connections.service.ts:62-72; email.service.ts:170-183, :207-221.
34	
35	### Swagger / OpenAPI generation
36	- `api/src/swagger.ts:3-21` builds the DocumentBuilder config (tags per surface — add a tag for a new surface here). `api/src/main.ts:24-25` serves docs at `/api/docs`. `api/src/openapi.ts:11-22` exports the spec to `api/openapi.json` **without listening** (that's why `isServing()` gates all cron/lifecycle side effects). Regen chain: root `package.json:16` `"openapi": "pnpm --filter robyn-api openapi && pnpm --filter robyn-web gen:types"`; api script `api/package.json:10` (`ts-node src/openapi.ts`); web script `web/package.json:10` (`openapi-typescript ../api/openapi.json -o lib/api-types.ts`). Any endpoint change ships in the same commit as regenerated spec + types (CLAUDE.md, API contract).
37	
38	### Global prefix / CORS — `api/src/main.ts`
39	- `app.setGlobalPrefix('api', { exclude: ['health'] })` (:14) — controllers use bare paths (`@Controller('meetings')`), URLs are `/api/...`. CORS is wide open: `NestFactory.create(AppModule, { cors: true })` (:12). Global `ValidationPipe` with `transform + whitelist + enableImplicitConversion` (:15-21). Port from `PORT` env, default 4000 (`env.ts:63`).
40	
41	### Other route surfaces (for chat tools / new endpoints)
42	- Tasks (read-only inbox): `GET /api/tasks`, `GET /api/tasks/all`, `GET /api/tasks/counts` (`tasks.controller.ts:11-31`; service comment "Tasks inbox is READ-ONLY... each card calls the owning module's endpoint" `tasks.service.ts:26-28`).
43	- Proposals: `GET /api/proposals`, `GET /api/proposals/:id`, `POST :id/approve|save-draft|reject`, `PATCH :id/lines` (`proposals.controller.ts:29-95`).
44	- Detections: `POST /api/detections/run`, `GET /api/detections`, `POST :id/propose`, `POST :id/dismiss` (`detections.controller.ts:36-89`).
45	- Clients: `GET /api/clients`, `GET :id`, `PATCH :id/autonomy`, `POST/GET :id/contract` (`clients.controller.ts:12-68`); potential clients: `GET /api/potential-clients`, `POST :id/confirm|dismiss` (`potential-clients.controller.ts:8-35`).
46	
47	---
48	
49	## 2. Frontend (web/, Next.js 14 app router)
50	
51	### Structure
52	- Route group `web/app/(dashboard)/` with surfaces: `page.tsx` (home = Tasks inbox), `calendar/`, `clients/`, `invoices/`, `connections/`, `tasks/`. Each surface keeps its client components in a local `_components/` dir. Shell: `web/app/(dashboard)/layout.tsx` — fixed 64-wide sidebar (`:20-22`), `TopBar`, persistent `LeakStrip` (`:26-29`), content max-w-[1200px].
53	
54	### Theme — where to flip the default to light
55	- `web/app/layout.tsx:43-48`: `<ThemeProvider attribute="class" defaultTheme="system" enableSystem disableTransitionOnChange>`. **Change `defaultTheme="system"` to `defaultTheme="light"` here** (optionally drop `enableSystem`). `web/components/theme-provider.tsx` is a thin next-themes wrapper (:6-11); the toggle is `web/components/mode-toggle.tsx`. Dark styles are `.dark` class-based (Tailwind).
56	
57	### Fetch layer — `web/lib/api.ts`
58	- `API_BASE = NEXT_PUBLIC_API_BASE ?? "http://localhost:4000/api"` (:12-13). `ApiError` with status+body (:16-25); `request()` never throws raw fetch errors (:27-76). Typed helpers: `apiGet` (OpenAPI-path-literal-typed GET, :101-106), loose `get/post/patch` (:109-127). Hooks: `useApi(path, {refreshMs})` — loading/error/isValidating/refetch, ticket-based race guard (:146-199); `useAction(fn)` for button mutations (:205-232). Comment at :6-9: **the ONLY source of types is `lib/api-types.ts`**, generated (header at api-types.ts:1-4), never hand-edited.
59	
60	### Calendar surface — `web/app/(dashboard)/calendar/_components/calendar-surface.tsx`
61	- Data hook: `useApi<CalendarEvent[]>("/dashboard/calendar")` (:101-102); event shape = `Schemas["CalendarEventDto"]` (:31) → `{id,title,start,end,state,colorKey,clientName,proposalId}` (see dashboard.service.ts:151-160). Month grid math :71-98; auto-centres on busiest month, fallback June 2026 (:85-98, :109-113). Colour tokens map through `colorKeyToTone`/`TONE_CLASS` from `web/lib/states.ts` (:8-14).
62	- Evidence panel: `EventSheet` (:262-524) fetches `useApi<MeetingDetail>("/meetings/:id")` (:271), renders attendees, transcript scope items with verbatim quotes (:353-385), proposal lines with `ProvenanceChip`s + policy reasons + Xero deep link (:389-455), and inline actions (attach transcript / skip) for AWAITING_TRANSCRIPT (:458-507). Note the `txt()` helper for openapi-typescript nullable strings (:34-38) — reuse it.
63	
64	### Connections surface
65	- `connections-surface.tsx`: `useApi<Row[]>("/connections", { refreshMs: 60_000 })` (:27-30), stable XERO/CALENDAR/EMAIL order (:19), full loading/error/empty handling (:65-87).
66	- `connection-card.tsx`: status badge via `connectionStatusMeta` (:102), human lead line per kind+status (:43-69), "Check now" routes per kind — XERO→`POST /connections/xero/recheck`, CALENDAR→`POST /meetings/sync`, EMAIL→`POST /email/poll` (:105-147), calendar card embeds `IcsImport` (:235-239), collapsible verbatim backend detail (:242-264). This is the pattern for any new provider card.
67	
68	### Tasks surface + router
69	- `tasks-surface.tsx`: `useApi<TaskDto[]>("/tasks", { refreshMs: 20_000 })` + `/dashboard/summary` (:23-27), refetch-both-after-resolve (:29-32).
70	- `task-router.tsx:13-28`: switch on `task.type` → `ProvideTranscriptCard | ConfirmClientCard | ReviewInvoiceCard | ConfirmAgreementCard | AttachContractCard` (one card component per `TaskType`, enums.ts:44-50). A new task type = new card file + a case here. Shared card plumbing in `_components/use-resolve.ts`, `context.ts`, `bits.tsx`.
71	
72	### Sidebar / nav — adding a Settings route
73	- `web/lib/nav.ts:19-30`: `NAV_ITEMS` array (five surfaces, home `/`). **Add `{ label: "Settings", href: "/settings", icon: Settings, matchPrefix: true }` here** (import icon from lucide-react, :1-8) plus a `web/app/(dashboard)/settings/page.tsx`; `isActive` handles prefix matching (:32-37). Rendered by `web/components/app-sidebar.tsx:35-51` (open-task badge only on `/`, :39).
74	
75	### UI primitives available
76	- `web/components/ui/`: badge, button, card, **dialog**, scroll-area, separator, **sheet**, skeleton, sonner (toasts), switch, tabs, textarea, tooltip. Shared: `PageHeader`, `LoadingState`/`ErrorState`/`EmptyState`, `StateBadge`, `ProvenanceChip`, `Money`, `RelativeTime`, `LeakStrip`, `SummaryStats`.
77	- **No chart library.** `web/package.json:12-30` — no recharts; deps are radix primitives, `framer-motion`, `lucide-react`, `next-themes`, `sonner`. Any dashboard charts either add recharts (new dep) or are hand-rolled SVG/CSS. CLAUDE.md: `impeccable` + `ui-ux-pro-max` skills mandatory for UI, `frontend-design` prohibited; Cursor owns `web/`.
78	
79	---
80	
81	## 3. Data available for dashboard charts + chat tools
82	
83	### Dashboard endpoints — `api/src/modules/dashboard/`
84	- `GET /api/dashboard/summary` → `{openTasks, unbilledGbp, invoicesSentThisMonth, clientsCount}` (dashboard.service.ts:255-284; unbilledGbp = sum of `Client.unbilledExposureGbp`).
85	- `GET /api/dashboard/leak-strip` → `{monthLabel, recoverableGbp, breakdown:[{source,gbp,count}]}` across OPEN detections + IN_REVIEW proposals + unbilled meetings (:164-237; unbilled meeting states listed :49-54, per-meeting £ estimate from client rate :94-103).
86	- `GET /api/dashboard/calendar?from&to` → `CalendarEventDto[]` with `colorKey` derived purely from meeting state (:77-91, :113-161).
87	- `GET /api/dashboard/audit?limit` → newest-first `AuditEventDto[]` (:240-252). Controller decorations at `dashboard.controller.ts:18-61`.
88	
89	### Queryable aggregates elsewhere
90	- Proposals list (`GET /api/proposals`) carries per-proposal `currency, subtotal, taxTotal, total, state, policyResult, lineCount, autoSent, xeroInvoiceNumber, xeroDeepLink` (`proposals/dto/proposal-response.dto.ts:16-67`) — good for invoice charts/chat.
91	- Detections (`GET /api/detections`) carry `type` (QUOTE_NOT_INVOICED / RETAINER_STOPPED / EXPENSE_NOT_RECHARGED), `valueGbp`, `evidence` jsonb, `state` (`entities/detection.entity.ts:9-31`, enums.ts:31-42).
92	- Tasks counts by type: `GET /api/tasks/counts` (`tasks.service.ts:60-72`).
93	- Meetings list with duration/state/client for utilisation charts (`meetings.service.ts:379-396`).
94	
95	### Xero data: cached locally vs fetched live — `api/src/modules/xero/xero-api.ts`
96	- **Read surface available**: `listContacts` (:33-38), `listInvoices(statuses?, page)` (:188-196), `getInvoice`/`findInvoiceByReference` (:120-131), `listPayments` (:259-262), `listQuotes(status)` (:298-303), `listAccounts`/`defaultSalesAccountCode` (:316-332), `getHistory`, `listAttachments`, and **`agedReceivablesByContact(contactId)` (:336-340) — implemented but currently UNUSED by any module** (free win for a dashboard/chat tool).
97	- **Cached locally in Postgres**: `Client.xeroContactId` + `unbilledExposureGbp` (client.entity.ts), `InvoiceProposal.xeroInvoiceId/Number/DeepLink` (invoice-proposal.entity.ts:43-55), `Detection` rows persist evidence derived from Xero reads. There is **no local cache table of Xero invoices/payments** — detections fetch them live at run time with graceful fallback to local fixtures on failure (detections.service.ts:165-176 quotes, :205-211 invoices, :288-291 payments).
98	- All access via `XeroService` (`xero.service.ts:8-52` — thin re-export of xero-api helpers plus `health()`); transport/auth/429/tenant in `xero-http.ts` (429 Retry-After retry :312-317, cached tenant :161-178, health :339-355).
99	
100	---
101	
102	## 4. Conventions that MUST be followed (violations get reverted)
103	
104	1. **DTO decoration**: every endpoint has `@ApiOperation` + `@ApiOkResponse`/`@ApiResponse`; every DTO field `@ApiProperty` (+ class-validator on inputs — global ValidationPipe at main.ts:15-21 whitelists). Example everywhere, e.g. meetings.controller.ts:32-37, proposal-response.dto.ts.
105	2. **Zod on every LLM output**: the LLM module pattern is `complete(system, user, zodSchema)` — parse JSON, `schema.parse`, retry once, then throw (never guess) (`llm.service.ts:41-73`, schemas in `llm/schemas.ts`). LLM parses/proposes at the edges only; **zero LLM calls inside engine decisions** (`engine/` is pure TS, unit-tested: matching/policy/proposal/state-machine `.spec.ts` files).
106	3. **AuditEvent on every write**: every verdict, approval, task raise/resolve, connection change, and Xero write records via `audit.record` (audit.service.ts:15-16 "No silent mutations anywhere").
107	4. **Idempotent Xero writes**: check-by-reference before create (`findInvoiceByReference` → `createInvoice` xero-api.ts:133-177; `ensureContact` :57-75; attachments overwrite-by-filename; stable `reference = ROBYN-<id8>` meetings.service.ts:264). Plus `idempotencyKey` headers and `assertElementOk` on `summarizeErrors=false` responses (:171-176). All Xero access through `api/src/modules/xero/` only.
108	5. **State-machine invariant**: "every transition either writes to Xero or raises a Task. No silent third path" (enums.ts:1-2; state-machine.ts:1-3). New behaviours must land as a state transition with a task or a Xero write, audited.
109	6. **OpenAPI regen discipline**: endpoint change ⇒ same commit includes regenerated `api/openapi.json` + `web/lib/api-types.ts` (`pnpm openapi`, root package.json:16). A frontend fetch with a hand-written type is a bug even if it works (CLAUDE.md). `web/lib/api-types.ts` is never hand-edited (api.ts:6-9).
110	7. Ownership: Claude Code owns `api/`, `seed/`, `.claude/skills/`; Cursor owns `web/`. OpenAPI contract is the only interface (CLAUDE.md).
111	
112	---
113	
114	## 5. Risk notes — do not trip over these
115	
116	- **React Strict Mode double-fetch**: `web/next.config.mjs:3` `reactStrictMode: true` → dev double-fires effects, so `useApi` fetches show duplicate 200s in DevTools (HANDOFF.md "Known rough edges"). Harmless, but don't "fix" it by removing StrictMode or adding fetch dedupe hacks; the `activeRef` ticket in `useApi` (api.ts:154-180) already prevents state races.
117	- **TypeORM `synchronize: true`, no migrations** (app.module.ts:32). New entity/column = auto schema change on boot. Never rename columns casually (data loss); adding nullable columns is safe. Not production-grade — known and accepted for the demo.
118	- **Port assumptions**: web is hardcoded to :3000 (`web/package.json:6` `next dev -p 3000`), API defaults to :4000 (env.ts:63), and the web fetch base defaults to `http://localhost:4000/api` (api.ts:12-13, override via `NEXT_PUBLIC_API_BASE`). The demo runbook and docs assume `http://localhost:3000` (HANDOFF.md "Run it"). CORS is fully open (`main.ts:12` `{ cors: true }`) so no origin lists to update, but keep the global `api` prefix (`main.ts:14`) in every new controller URL you hand to the frontend.
119	- **OpenAPI export instantiates the full module graph** (openapi.ts:14) — any new constructor/lifecycle side effect (cron, pollers, eager connects) MUST be gated on `isServing()` (`common/bootstrap-flag.ts`), or `pnpm openapi` will fire it. The existing calendar cron shows the pattern (meetings.controller.ts:114-118). Note `LlmService`'s constructor already requires `ANTHROPIC_API_KEY` at module build (llm.service.ts:28 via loadConfig) — the export needs a populated `api/.env`.
120	- **Xero rate limit (60/min)**: retries on 429 exist (xero-http.ts:312-317) but don't poll Xero in loops; batch reads and prefer local DB rows for dashboards (CLAUDE.md backend rules). Detections already model the "live read with local fallback" pattern (detections.service.ts:165-176).
121	- **`meetings/sync` cron every 15 min** re-runs Loop-1 on unprogressed meetings (meetings.controller.ts:114-118, runEligible meetings.service.ts:537-563) — anything you add to Loop-1 must stay idempotent (task dedupeKey, proposal reference) because it WILL re-run.
122	- **Fallback truthfulness**: CALENDAR/EMAIL FALLBACK states are honest demo modes, not errors (HANDOFF.md "Fallbacks"). Never render FALLBACK as broken, and never fake LIVE.
123	- Nullable strings from openapi-typescript arrive as `string | Record<string, never>` — use the `txt()` guard pattern (calendar-surface.tsx:34-38) in new UI.
124
```
</details></tool-use>

14	// Aggregate read surfaces: the calendar-view feed (events colour-coded by

16	// headline summary. Read-only — every mutation lives in its owning module.

18	  imports: [
19	    TypeOrmModule.forFeature([Meeting, Client, InvoiceProposal, Detection, Task, AuditEvent]),
20	  ],
21	  providers: [DashboardService],
22	  controllers: [DashboardController],
23	  exports: [TypeOrmModule],
24	})
25	export class DashboardModule {}

```text
1	# Data-feasibility map: agentic finance chat + Xero-style dashboard charts
2	
3	All paths relative to `api/src/`. Line numbers from current `main` (commit 915752e).
4	
5	---
6	
7	## 1. Chat tool inventory (read-only, over existing services)
8	
9	All candidate tools call existing injectable services. XeroModule is `@Global()` (`modules/xero/xero.module.ts:6`), so a new ChatModule gets `XeroService` for free; DB-backed tools need the relevant `TypeOrmModule.forFeature` repos or the owning service exported.
10	
11	| # | Tool | Backing call | Input params | Output shape | New code needed? |
12	|---|------|--------------|--------------|--------------|------------------|
13	| 1 | `dashboard_summary` | `DashboardService.summary()` — `modules/dashboard/dashboard.service.ts:255-284` | none | `{openTasks, unbilledGbp, invoicesSentThisMonth, clientsCount}` | No |
14	| 2 | `leak_breakdown` | `DashboardService.leakStrip()` — `dashboard.service.ts:164-237` | none | `{monthLabel, recoverableGbp, breakdown:[{source,gbp,count}x3]}` (detections + in-review proposals + unbilled meetings) | No |
15	| 3 | `list_proposals` | `ProposalsService.list(state?)` — `modules/proposals/proposals.service.ts:66-78` | `state?: DRAFT\|IN_REVIEW\|APPROVED\|SENT\|REJECTED` (`entities/enums.ts:23-29`) | `ProposalSummaryDto[]` (clientName, total, state, reference, xeroDeepLink — mapped at `proposals.service.ts:416-441`) | No |
16	| 4 | `proposal_detail` | `ProposalsService.detail(id)` — `proposals.service.ts:80-167` | `id: uuid` | summary + `lines[]` + evidence chain (meeting/transcript/detection/contract) + live `xeroStatus` | No |
17	| 5 | `list_detections` | `DetectionsService.list(state?, type?)` — `modules/detections/detections.service.ts:433-459` | `state?: OPEN\|PROPOSED\|RESOLVED\|DISMISSED`, `type?:` 3 detector types (`enums.ts:32-43`) | `DetectionDto[]` (valueGbp, summary, source, proposalId link) | No |
18	| 6 | `open_tasks` | `TasksService.listOpen()` — `modules/tasks/tasks.service.ts:44-50`; badge counts via `counts()` — `tasks.service.ts:61-70` | none | `TaskDto[]` enriched with client/meeting/proposal context + resolution endpoint hint (`tasks.service.ts:81-96`) | No |
19	| 7 | `list_meetings` | `MeetingsService.list(state?)` — `modules/meetings/meetings.service.ts:379-396`; ranged calendar via `DashboardService.calendar(from,to)` — `dashboard.service.ts:113-161` | `state?: MeetingState` or `from/to` ISO dates | `MeetingListItemDto[]` / `CalendarEventDto[]` (colorKey, clientName, proposalId) | No |
20	| 8 | `meeting_utilisation` | aggregate over `Meeting` repo: group by `state` (`enums.ts:4-13`), sum `durationHours` (`entities/meeting.entity.ts` numeric col), billed = SENT, unbilled = `UNBILLED_MEETING_STATES` (`dashboard.service.ts:49-54`) | `month?: YYYY-MM` | `{month, billedHours, unbilledHours, personalHours, byState[]}` | **Yes — small new query method** (one `find` + reduce, pattern identical to `leakStrip`) |
21	| 9 | `client_revenue` | aggregate: `InvoiceProposal` where `state=SENT` grouped by `clientId`, sum `total` (`entities/invoice-proposal.entity.ts` cols `clientId`, `total`, `state`); names via `ClientsService.listClients()` — `modules/clients/clients.service.ts:54` | `topN?: number` | `[{clientId, clientName, sentGbp, proposalCount}]` | **Yes — small new query method** |
22	| 10 | `list_unpaid_invoices` (live Xero) | `XeroService.listInvoices(['AUTHORISED','SUBMITTED'])` — `modules/xero/xero-api.ts:188-196`; fields `AmountDue`, `DueDate`, `Status`, `Total` on `XeroInvoice` (`xero-api.ts:89-106`) | `statuses?: string[]`, `page?: number` | `{invoices: XeroInvoice[], pagination}` — compute `overdue = DueDate < today` in the tool wrapper | No (overdue flag computed in wrapper) |
23	| 11 | `payments_received` (live Xero) | `XeroService.listPayments()` — `xero-api.ts:259-262` (`XeroPayment {Date, Amount, Reference, Invoice}` `xero-api.ts:250-257`) | `page?: number` | payment list; group by month in wrapper | No (but see the `/Date(ms)/` parsing caveat in §3 chart 2) |
24	| 12 | `aged_receivables_live` | `XeroService.agedReceivablesByContact(contactId)` — `xero-api.ts:336-340` (raw report envelope, typed `any`) | `clientId: uuid` → resolve `Client.xeroContactId` (`entities/client.entity.ts` unique col) | Xero report Rows envelope | No, but wrapper must map clientId→contactId and handle `xeroContactId=null` |
25	| 13 | `audit_trail` | `DashboardService.auditTrail(limit)` — `dashboard.service.ts:240-252` / `AuditService.list(limit=200)` — `modules/audit/audit.service.ts:36` | `limit?: number` | `AuditEventDto[]` newest-first | No |
26	
27	Guard-rails: tools 10-12 hit Xero live — respect the 60/min budget (retry/429 handling already inside `xeroRequest`, `modules/xero/xero-http.ts:304-331`); probe `XeroService.health()` (`modules/xero/xero.service.ts:43-47`) once per chat turn and degrade to DB-backed tools when not live (same pattern as `DetectionsService.runDetectors`, `detections.service.ts:82-87`). Every tool call should write an `AuditEvent` via `AuditService.record` (`audit.service.ts`) per the no-silent-reads-into-actions rule.
28	
29	---
30	
31	## 2. Existing Anthropic integration (`modules/llm/`)
32	
33	- **SDK, not raw fetch:** `import Anthropic from '@anthropic-ai/sdk'` — `llm.service.ts:2`; version `^0.68.0` (`api/package.json:18`). Client built once in the constructor with `loadConfig().anthropicApiKey` (`llm.service.ts:27-29`; env assert at `config/env.ts:65`).
34	- **Model:** `const MODEL = 'claude-sonnet-5'` — `llm.service.ts:20`. Single constant; chat service should import/share it.
35	- **Call shape:** non-streaming `this.client.messages.create({model, max_tokens, system, messages:[single user turn]})` — `llm.service.ts:49-62`. Per-call `max_tokens`: 800-2500 (default 2000, `llm.service.ts:45`).
36	- **Validation pattern:** `complete<S extends z.ZodTypeAny>()` (`llm.service.ts:41-73`) — extract JSON from the text block (`extractJson`, `llm.service.ts:31-39`, tolerates fences/prose), `schema.parse(...)`, retry ONCE with a corrective suffix, then throw. All schemas in `modules/llm/schemas.ts` (zod, "nothing unvalidated crosses this boundary", `schemas.ts:3-4`).
37	- **Health probe:** 8-token ping (`llm.service.ts:144-156`).
38	
39	**Missing for multi-turn tool-use chat (what a ChatService adds on top of this plumbing):**
40	1. **No `tools` parameter anywhere** — no tool definitions, no `tool_use`/`tool_result` loop. SDK 0.68.0 supports the manual loop (`stop_reason === 'tool_use'` → execute → push `tool_result` blocks in ONE user message) and the beta tool runner (`client.beta.messages.toolRunner` + `betaZodTool` from `@anthropic-ai/sdk/helpers/beta/zod`) — the zod-per-tool-input style matches the project's existing zod discipline.
41	2. **No streaming** — everything is `messages.create`. For chat use `client.messages.stream(...)` (iterate `content_block_delta` / `text` events, `finalMessage()` for the complete turn) or `toolRunner({..., stream: true})`; surface to the frontend as SSE from a Nest controller (`@Sse()` or raw `res.write`), which also satisfies the "optimistic/streaming where the agent is thinking" frontend rule.
42	3. **No conversation state** — `complete()` sends one user turn. Chat needs message-history assembly (client-supplied history is fine for demo; a `ChatMessage` entity is optional) and the tool_use/tool_result blocks appended verbatim between turns.
43	4. **No system prompt for chat persona/tool policy** — the four existing prompts are extraction-specific (`llm.service.ts:76-142`).
44	
45	Reuse: the `Anthropic` client instance (export it or add a `chat()` method on `LlmService`), the `MODEL` constant, the zod-validate-everything pattern, and the audit-on-LLM-failure pattern (`AuditActor.LLM`, e.g. `meetings.service.ts:140-148`).
46	
47	---
48	
49	## 3. Charts feasibility (Xero-dashboard-style board)
50	
51	XeroModule is global, so `DashboardService` can inject `XeroService` without module changes (`xero.module.ts:6-10`); all four charts fit as new methods on `dashboard.service.ts` + new GET endpoints on `dashboard.controller.ts` (+ regenerated `openapi.json` / `web/lib/api-types.ts` per the contract rule).
52	
53	### Chart 1 — Invoices owed (draft / awaiting payment / overdue, with £)
54	- **Live source:** `XeroService.listInvoices(['DRAFT','SUBMITTED','AUTHORISED'])` (`xero-api.ts:188-196`). Buckets: `Status==='DRAFT'||'SUBMITTED'` → draft (sum `Total`); `AUTHORISED && DueDate >= today` → awaiting payment (sum `AmountDue`); `AUTHORISED && DueDate < today` → overdue (sum `AmountDue`). Fields on `XeroInvoice` at `xero-api.ts:96-104`.
55	- **Local fallback (Xero down):** `InvoiceProposal` by `state` — DRAFT/IN_REVIEW → draft, SENT → awaiting (sum `total`); no overdue signal locally.
56	- **Wiring:** new `GET /dashboard/invoices-owed` extending `dashboard.service.ts`. One Xero page read per load — cache-friendly, fits the 60/min rule.
57	
58	### Chart 2 — Cash in per month (payments received)
59	- **Live source:** `XeroService.listPayments()` (`xero-api.ts:259-262`), group `Date` by `YYYY-MM`, sum `Amount`.
60	- **Caveat:** the Accounting API returns JSON dates as `/Date(1718150400000+0000)/` — `XeroPayment.Date` is typed `string` (`xero-api.ts:252`); the aggregation needs a tiny `parseXeroDate()` helper (regex the epoch ms). Nothing in the codebase parses this yet — flagged as new code.
61	- **Local fallback:** SENT `InvoiceProposal.createdAt` by month (the retainer detector already does exactly this month-keying — `detections.service.ts:273-282`, `monthKey` helper `detections.service.ts:735-737`).
62	- **Wiring:** new `GET /dashboard/cash-in` extending `dashboard.service`.
63	
64	### Chart 3 — Money Robyn found (recovered vs pending)
65	- **Source (all local, already injected in DashboardService — `dashboard.service.ts:65-66`):** `Detection` rows: pending = `state IN (OPEN, PROPOSED)` sum `valueGbp`; recovered = `state=RESOLVED` sum `valueGbp` **plus** detection-sourced proposals that reached SENT (join `InvoiceProposal.detectionId` — the map pattern already exists at `detections.service.ts:446-450`). Dismissed excluded.
66	- **Wiring:** extend `dashboard.service` (no new repos needed); new `GET /dashboard/robyn-found`. Optionally split by `DetectionType` for a stacked bar.
67	
68	### Chart 4 — Unbilled pipeline
69	- **Source:** exactly the `leakStrip()` inputs (`dashboard.service.ts:165-199`): open detections £ + IN_REVIEW proposals £ + unbilled-meeting estimates (`estimateMeetingValue`, `dashboard.service.ts:94-103`). Per-client variant: group the same rows by `clientId`, or use the denormalised `Client.unbilledExposureGbp` (`entities/client.entity.ts`, already summed in `summary()` at `dashboard.service.ts:273-276`).
70	- **Wiring:** the totals endpoint already exists (`GET /dashboard/leak-strip` returns the 3-part breakdown — chartable as-is with **zero backend work**); a per-client stacked version is a small extension of `leakStrip()`.
71	
72	Summary: charts 3 and 4 are pure-DB and near-free; charts 1 and 2 are live-Xero reads (one API call each, cache/If-Modified-Since friendly) plus small aggregation methods. All four belong in `dashboard.service.ts` per the existing "aggregate read surface" comment (`dashboard.module.ts:14-16`).
73	
74	---
75	
76	## 4. Agent-settings persistence
77	
78	- **Closest existing pattern:** `ConnectionState` (`entities/connection-state.entity.ts`) — a one-row-per-kind config table with a unique-indexed key column, extending `BaseEntity` (uuid PK + `createdAt`/`updatedAt`, `entities/base.entity.ts`). Upsert pattern in `MeetingsService.upsertCalendarConnection` (`meetings.service.ts:924-932`): find-by-kind, create-if-missing, save.
79	- **Recommended shape:** `AgentSetting { @Index({unique:true}) key: varchar; value: jsonb }` — use a plain `varchar` key, **not** a Postgres enum column: with `synchronize: true` (`app.module.ts:35`, "hackathon: schema auto-managed. No migrations") a **new table/column auto-creates on boot**, but altering an existing enum's members is where TypeORM synchronize gets flaky — varchar keys avoid that entirely. Register the entity in `ALL_ENTITIES` (`entities/index.ts:27-37`) or it won't be created.
80	- **Per-client autonomy already exists** and is the model precedent: `Client.autonomyEnabled` (`entities/client.entity.ts`) toggled via `ClientsService.setAutonomy` (`clients.service.ts:93`) — global chat-agent settings (e.g. `chat.model`, `chat.max_tool_calls`, `chat.xero_live_tools_enabled`) should mirror that but in the key-value table.
81	- Settings mutations must write an `AuditEvent` (`entities/audit-event.entity.ts:5-6`: "no silent mutations").
82
```

<tool-use data-tool-type="read" data-tool-name="Read"><details>
<summary>Tool use: **Read**</summary>
 `./api/src/modules/dashboard/dashboard.service.ts`

```text
1	import { Injectable } from '@nestjs/common';
2	import { InjectRepository } from '@nestjs/typeorm';
3	import {
4	  Between,
5	  FindOptionsWhere,
6	  In,
7	  LessThanOrEqual,
8	  MoreThanOrEqual,
9	  Repository,
10	} from 'typeorm';
11	import {
12	  Client,
13	  Detection,
14	  DetectionState,
15	  InvoiceProposal,
16	  InvoiceProposalState,
17	  Meeting,
18	  MeetingState,
19	  Task,
20	  TaskState,
21	} from '../../entities';
22	import { AuditService } from '../audit/audit.service';
23	import {
24	  AuditEventDto,
25	  CalendarColorKey,
26	  CalendarEventDto,
27	  DashboardSummaryDto,
28	  LeakStripDto,
29	} from './dto/dashboard.dto';
30	
31	const MONTH_NAMES = [
32	  'January',
33	  'February',
34	  'March',
35	  'April',
36	  'May',
37	  'June',
38	  'July',
39	  'August',
40	  'September',
41	  'October',
42	  'November',
43	  'December',
44	];
45	
46	// Meeting states whose work is still recoverable (not yet SENT / SKIPPED) and
47	// that do NOT already carry a proposal (INVOICE_PROPOSED is counted in the
48	// in-review bucket instead, so it is excluded here to avoid double counting).
49	const UNBILLED_MEETING_STATES: MeetingState[] = [
50	  MeetingState.DETECTED,
51	  MeetingState.AWAITING_TRANSCRIPT,
52	  MeetingState.TRANSCRIPT_ATTACHED,
53	  MeetingState.CLIENT_MATCHED,
54	];
55	
56	@Injectable()
57	export class DashboardService {
58	  constructor(
59	    @InjectRepository(Meeting)
60	    private readonly meetings: Repository<Meeting>,
61	    @InjectRepository(Client)
62	    private readonly clients: Repository<Client>,
63	    @InjectRepository(InvoiceProposal)
64	    private readonly proposals: Repository<InvoiceProposal>,
65	    @InjectRepository(Detection)
66	    private readonly detections: Repository<Detection>,
67	    @InjectRepository(Task)
68	    private readonly tasks: Repository<Task>,
69	    private readonly audit: AuditService,
70	  ) {}
71	
72	  private static round2(n: number): number {
73	    return Math.round((n + Number.EPSILON) * 100) / 100;
74	  }
75	
76	  // Colour token for the calendar view, derived purely from meeting state.
77	  private colorKeyFor(m: Meeting): CalendarColorKey {
78	    if (m.isPersonal) return 'gray';
79	    switch (m.state) {
80	      case MeetingState.SENT:
81	        return 'teal'; // billed
82	      case MeetingState.INVOICE_PROPOSED:
83	        return 'amber'; // proposal in review
84	      case MeetingState.UNKNOWN_ATTENDEE:
85	        return 'purple'; // unknown attendee
86	      case MeetingState.SKIPPED:
87	        return 'gray'; // skipped
88	      default:
89	        return 'amber-outline'; // awaiting (detected / awaiting transcript / attached / matched)
90	    }
91	  }
92	
93	  // Best-effort £ estimate of a single unbilled meeting from its client's rate.
94	  private estimateMeetingValue(m: Meeting, client?: Client): number {
95	    const bp = client?.billingProfile;
96	    if (!bp || bp.rate == null) return 0;
97	    const rate = Number(bp.rate);
98	    const hours = Number(m.durationHours);
99	    if (!Number.isFinite(rate) || !Number.isFinite(hours)) return 0;
100	    if (bp.rate_unit === 'HOUR') return rate * hours;
101	    if (bp.rate_unit === 'DAY') return rate * (hours / 8); // ~8h working day
102	    return 0; // FIXED / unknown — not estimable per meeting
103	  }
104	
105	  private async clientsById(ids: string[]): Promise<Map<string, Client>> {
106	    const unique = [...new Set(ids.filter(Boolean))];
107	    if (unique.length === 0) return new Map();
108	    const rows = await this.clients.find({ where: { id: In(unique) } });
109	    return new Map(rows.map((c) => [c.id, c]));
110	  }
111	
112	  // GET /dashboard/calendar — colour-coded events with client + proposal links.
113	  async calendar(from?: string, to?: string): Promise<CalendarEventDto[]> {
114	    const where: FindOptionsWhere<Meeting> = {};
115	    if (from && to) {
116	      where.start = Between(new Date(from), new Date(to));
117	    } else if (from) {
118	      where.start = MoreThanOrEqual(new Date(from));
119	    } else if (to) {
120	      where.start = LessThanOrEqual(new Date(to));
121	    }
122	
123	    const meetings = await this.meetings.find({
124	      where,
125	      order: { start: 'ASC' },
126	    });
127	    if (meetings.length === 0) return [];
128	
129	    const clientMap = await this.clientsById(
130	      meetings.map((m) => m.clientId ?? '').filter(Boolean),
131	    );
132	
133	    // Map each meeting to its most recent proposal (prefer non-rejected).
134	    const meetingIds = meetings.map((m) => m.id);
135	    const proposalRows = await this.proposals.find({
136	      where: { meetingId: In(meetingIds) },
137	      order: { createdAt: 'DESC' },
138	    });
139	    const proposalByMeeting = new Map<string, string>();
140	    for (const p of proposalRows) {
141	      if (!p.meetingId) continue;
142	      const existing = proposalByMeeting.get(p.meetingId);
143	      if (!existing) {
144	        proposalByMeeting.set(p.meetingId, p.id);
145	      } else if (p.state !== InvoiceProposalState.REJECTED) {
146	        // A newer non-rejected proposal wins over an older rejected one.
147	        proposalByMeeting.set(p.meetingId, p.id);
148	      }
149	    }
150	
151	    return meetings.map((m) => ({
152	      id: m.id,
153	      title: m.title,
154	      start: m.start.toISOString(),
155	      end: m.end.toISOString(),
156	      state: m.state,
157	      colorKey: this.colorKeyFor(m),
158	      clientName: m.clientId ? (clientMap.get(m.clientId)?.name ?? null) : null,
159	      proposalId: proposalByMeeting.get(m.id) ?? null,
160	    }));
161	  }
162	
163	  // GET /dashboard/leak-strip — "£2,880 was walking away" this month.
164	  async leakStrip(): Promise<LeakStripDto> {
165	    const [openDetections, inReviewProposals, unbilledMeetings] =
166	      await Promise.all([
167	        this.detections.find({ where: { state: DetectionState.OPEN } }),
168	        this.proposals.find({
169	          where: { state: InvoiceProposalState.IN_REVIEW },
170	        }),
171	        this.meetings.find({
172	          where: {
173	            state: In(UNBILLED_MEETING_STATES),
174	            isPersonal: false,
175	          },
176	        }),
177	      ]);
178	
179	    const detectionGbp = openDetections.reduce(
180	      (sum, d) => sum + Number(d.valueGbp),
181	      0,
182	    );
183	    const reviewGbp = inReviewProposals.reduce(
184	      (sum, p) => sum + Number(p.total),
185	      0,
186	    );
187	
188	    const meetingClientMap = await this.clientsById(
189	      unbilledMeetings.map((m) => m.clientId ?? '').filter(Boolean),
190	    );
191	    const meetingGbp = unbilledMeetings.reduce(
192	      (sum, m) =>
193	        sum +
194	        this.estimateMeetingValue(
195	          m,
196	          m.clientId ? meetingClientMap.get(m.clientId) : undefined,
197	        ),
198	      0,
199	    );
200	
201	    const breakdown = [
202	      {
203	        source: 'Ledger detections',
204	        gbp: DashboardService.round2(detectionGbp),
205	        count: openDetections.length,
206	      },
207	      {
208	        source: 'Invoices in review',
209	        gbp: DashboardService.round2(reviewGbp),
210	        count: inReviewProposals.length,
211	      },
212	      {
213	        source: 'Unbilled meetings',
214	        gbp: DashboardService.round2(meetingGbp),
215	        count: unbilledMeetings.length,
216	      },
217	    ];
218	
219	    // Label the strip with the month of the most recent unbilled meeting so
220	    // the demo reads "June 2026"; fall back to the current month.
221	    let labelDate = new Date();
222	    if (unbilledMeetings.length > 0) {
223	      labelDate = unbilledMeetings.reduce(
224	        (latest, m) => (m.start > latest ? m.start : latest),
225	        unbilledMeetings[0].start,
226	      );
227	    }
228	    const monthLabel = `${MONTH_NAMES[labelDate.getMonth()]} ${labelDate.getFullYear()}`;
229	
230	    return {
231	      monthLabel,
232	      recoverableGbp: DashboardService.round2(
233	        detectionGbp + reviewGbp + meetingGbp,
234	      ),
235	      breakdown,
236	    };
237	  }
238	
239	  // GET /dashboard/audit — the audit trail feed (newest first).
240	  async auditTrail(limit = 100): Promise<AuditEventDto[]> {
241	    const events = await this.audit.list(limit);
242	    return events.map((e) => ({
243	      id: e.id,
244	      actor: e.actor,
245	      action: e.action,
246	      summary: e.summary,
247	      subjectType: e.subjectType,
248	      subjectId: e.subjectId,
249	      inputs: e.inputs,
250	      createdAt: e.createdAt.toISOString(),
251	    }));
252	  }
253	
254	  // GET /dashboard/summary — the headline stats row.
255	  async summary(): Promise<DashboardSummaryDto> {
256	    const now = new Date();
257	    const monthStart = new Date(now.getFullYear(), now.getMonth(), 1);
258	    const monthEnd = new Date(now.getFullYear(), now.getMonth() + 1, 1);
259	
260	    const [openTasks, clientRows, invoicesSentThisMonth, clientsCount] =
261	      await Promise.all([
262	        this.tasks.count({ where: { state: TaskState.OPEN } }),
263	        this.clients.find(),
264	        this.proposals.count({
265	          where: {
266	            state: InvoiceProposalState.SENT,
267	            updatedAt: Between(monthStart, monthEnd),
268	          },
269	        }),
270	        this.clients.count(),
271	      ]);
272	
273	    const unbilledGbp = clientRows.reduce(
274	      (sum, c) => sum + Number(c.unbilledExposureGbp),
275	      0,
276	    );
277	
278	    return {
279	      openTasks,
280	      unbilledGbp: DashboardService.round2(unbilledGbp),
281	      invoicesSentThisMonth,
282	      clientsCount,
283	    };
284	  }
285	}
286
```

# Building LLM-Powered Applications with Claude

This skill helps you build LLM-powered applications with Claude. Choose the right surface based on your needs, detect the project language, then read the relevant language-specific documentation.

## Before You Start

Scan the target file (or, if no target file, the prompt and project) for non-Anthropic provider markers — `import openai`, `from openai`, `langchain_openai`, `OpenAI(`, `gpt-4`, `gpt-5`, file names like `agent-openai.py` or `*-generic.py`, or any explicit instruction to keep the code provider-neutral. If you find any, stop and tell the user that this skill produces Claude/Anthropic SDK code; ask whether they want to switch the file to Claude or want a non-Claude implementation. Do not edit a non-Anthropic file with Anthropic SDK calls.

## Output Requirement

When the user asks you to add, modify, or implement a Claude feature, your code must call Claude through one of:

1. **The official Anthropic SDK** for the project's language (`anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai/sdk`, `com.anthropic.*`, etc.). This is the default whenever a supported SDK exists for the project.
2. **Raw HTTP** (`curl`, `requests`, `fetch`, `httpx`, etc.) — only when the user explicitly asks for cURL/REST/raw HTTP, the project is a shell/cURL project, or the language has no official SDK.

Never mix the two — don't reach for `requests`/`fetch` in a Python or TypeScript project just because it feels lighter. Never fall back to OpenAI-compatible shims.

**Never guess SDK usage.** Function names, class names, namespaces, method signatures, and import paths must come from explicit documentation — either the `{lang}/` files in this skill or the official SDK repositories or documentation links listed in `shared/live-sources.md`. If the binding you need is not explicitly documented in the skill files, WebFetch the relevant SDK repo from `shared/live-sources.md` before writing code. Do not infer Ruby/Java/Go/PHP/C# APIs from cURL shapes or from another language's SDK.

**If WebFetch or repository access fails** (network restricted, timeouts, clone blocked): do not keep retrying — write code from the patterns and namespace/package tables in the `{lang}/` file, run the compiler or interpreter on it, and iterate on the error output. For statically-typed SDKs (C#, Java, Go) a compile-fix loop against local errors reaches working code faster than blocked network research.

## Defaults

Unless the user requests otherwise:

For the Claude model version, please use Claude Opus 4.8, which you can access via the exact model string `claude-opus-4-8`. Please default to using adaptive thinking (`thinking: {type: "adaptive"}`) for anything remotely complicated. And finally, please default to streaming for any request that may involve long input, long output, or high `max_tokens` — it prevents hitting request timeouts. Use the SDK's `.get_final_message()` / `.finalMessage()` helper to get the complete response if you don't need to handle individual stream events

## ⚠️ API Drift — Your Training Prior May Be Stale

Several common Claude API shapes changed in 2025–2026. If you recall a pattern from training, verify it against the `{lang}/` files in this skill before writing — the rows below are the most frequent drift points:

| Area | Stale prior | Current API |
|---|---|---|
| Extended thinking | `thinking: {type: "enabled", budget_tokens: N}` | On Claude 4.6+ models: `thinking: {type: "adaptive"}`. `budget_tokens` is deprecated on Opus 4.6 / Sonnet 4.6 and **rejected with a 400** on Fable 5 / Sonnet 5 / Opus 4.8 / 4.7. Pre-4.6 models still use `budget_tokens`. |
| Web search / web fetch tool type | `web_search_20250305`, `web_fetch_20250910` | `web_search_20260209`, `web_fetch_20260209` (dynamic filtering) on Opus 4.8/4.7/4.6, Sonnet 5, and Sonnet 4.6. Older models keep the basic variants; on Vertex AI only basic `web_search_20250305` is available (web fetch is not on Vertex) — see the Server Tools QR below. |
| PHP parameter names | snake_case wire names as named args (`max_tokens`) | Top-level named args are camelCase (`maxTokens`). Nested array keys vary by feature (e.g. `'taskBudget'`, `'skillID'`, `'mcp_server_name'`) — copy the exact key from the documented example; do not bulk-convert. |

The `{lang}/` files in this skill are authoritative over recalled patterns.

---

## Subcommands

If the User Request at the bottom of this prompt is a bare subcommand string (no prose), search every **Subcommands** table in this document — including any in sections appended below — and follow the matching Action column directly. This lets users invoke specific flows via `/claude-api <subcommand>`. If no table in the document matches, treat the request as normal prose.

| Subcommand | Action |
|---|---|
| `migrate` | Migrate existing Claude API code to a newer model. **Read `shared/model-migration.md` immediately** and follow it in order: Step 0 (confirm scope — ask which files/directories before any edit), Step 1 (classify each file), then the per-target breaking-changes section. Do not summarize the guide — execute it. If the user did not name a target model, ask which model to migrate to in the same turn as the scope question. |

---

## Language Detection

Before reading code examples, determine which language the user is working in:

1. **Look at project files** to infer the language:

   - `*.py`, `requirements.txt`, `pyproject.toml`, `setup.py`, `Pipfile` → **Python** — read from `python/`
   - `*.ts`, `*.tsx`, `package.json`, `tsconfig.json` → **TypeScript** — read from `typescript/`
   - `*.js`, `*.jsx` (no `.ts` files present) → **TypeScript** — JS uses the same SDK, read from `typescript/`
   - `*.java`, `pom.xml`, `build.gradle` → **Java** — read from `java/`
   - `*.kt`, `*.kts`, `build.gradle.kts` → **Java** — Kotlin uses the Java SDK, read from `java/`
   - `*.scala`, `build.sbt` → **Java** — Scala uses the Java SDK, read from `java/`
   - `*.go`, `go.mod` → **Go** — read from `go/`
   - `*.rb`, `Gemfile` → **Ruby** — read from `ruby/`
   - `*.cs`, `*.csproj` → **C#** — read from `csharp/`
   - `*.php`, `composer.json` → **PHP** — read from `php/`

2. **If multiple languages detected** (e.g., both Python and TypeScript files):

   - Check which language the user's current file or question relates to
   - If still ambiguous, ask: "I detected both Python and TypeScript files. Which language are you using for the Claude API integration?"

3. **If language can't be inferred** (empty project, no source files, or unsupported language):

   - Use AskUserQuestion with options: Python, TypeScript, Java, Go, Ruby, cURL/raw HTTP, C#, PHP
   - If AskUserQuestion is unavailable, default to Python examples and note: "Showing Python examples. Let me know if you need a different language."

4. **If unsupported language detected** (Rust, Swift, C++, Elixir, etc.):

   - Suggest cURL/raw HTTP examples from `curl/` and note that community SDKs may exist
   - Offer to show Python or TypeScript examples as reference implementations

5. **If user needs cURL/raw HTTP examples**, read from `curl/`.

### Language-Specific Feature Support

| Language   | Tool Runner | Managed Agents | Notes                                 |
| ---------- | ----------- | -------------- | ------------------------------------- |
| Python     | Yes (beta)  | Yes (beta)     | Full support — `@beta_tool` decorator |
| TypeScript | Yes (beta)  | Yes (beta)     | Full support — `betaZodTool` + Zod    |
| Java       | Yes (beta)  | Yes (beta)     | Beta tool use with annotated classes  |
| Go         | Yes (beta)  | Yes (beta)     | `BetaToolRunner` in `toolrunner` pkg  |
| Ruby       | Yes (beta)  | Yes (beta)     | `BaseTool` + `tool_runner` in beta    |
| C#         | Yes (beta)  | Yes (beta)     | `BetaToolRunner` + raw JSON schema    |
| PHP        | Yes (beta)  | Yes (beta)     | `BetaRunnableTool` + `toolRunner()`   |
| cURL       | N/A         | Yes (beta)     | Raw HTTP, no SDK features             |

> **Managed Agents code examples**: dedicated language-specific READMEs are provided for Python, TypeScript, Go, Ruby, PHP, Java, and cURL (`{lang}/managed-agents/README.md`, `curl/managed-agents.md`). Read your language's README plus the language-agnostic `shared/managed-agents-*.md` concept files. **Agents are persistent — create once, reference by ID.** Store the agent ID returned by `agents.create` and pass it to every subsequent `sessions.create`; do not call `agents.create` in the request path. The Anthropic CLI (`ant`) is one convenient way to create agents and environments from version-controlled YAML — see `shared/anthropic-cli.md`. If a binding you need isn't shown in the README, WebFetch the relevant entry from `shared/live-sources.md` rather than guess. C# has beta Managed Agents support via `client.Beta.Agents` and related namespaces.

---

## Which Surface Should I Use?

> **Start simple.** Default to the simplest tier that meets your needs. Single API calls and workflows handle most use cases — only reach for agents when the task genuinely requires open-ended, model-driven exploration.

| Use Case                                        | Tier            | Recommended Surface       | Why                                                          |
| ----------------------------------------------- | --------------- | ------------------------- | ------------------------------------------------------------ |
| Classification, summarization, extraction, Q&A  | Single LLM call | **Claude API**            | One request, one response                                    |
| Batch processing or embeddings                  | Single LLM call | **Claude API**            | Specialized endpoints                                        |
| Multi-step pipelines with code-controlled logic | Workflow        | **Claude API + tool use** | You orchestrate the loop                                     |
| Custom agent with your own tools                | Agent           | **Claude API + tool use** | Maximum flexibility                                          |
| Server-managed stateful agent with workspace    | Agent           | **Managed Agents**        | Anthropic runs the loop and hosts the tool-execution sandbox |
| Persisted, versioned agent configs              | Agent           | **Managed Agents**        | Agents are stored objects; sessions pin to a version         |
| Long-running multi-turn agent with file mounts  | Agent           | **Managed Agents**        | Per-session containers, SSE event stream, Skills + MCP       |

> **Note:** Managed Agents is the right choice when you want Anthropic to run the agent loop *and* host the container where tools execute — file ops, bash, code execution all run in the per-session workspace. If you want to host the compute yourself or run your own custom tool runtime, Claude API + tool use is the right choice — use the tool runner for automatic loop handling, or the manual loop for fine-grained control (approval gates, custom logging, conditional execution).

> **Cloud-provider access.** **Claude Platform on AWS** is Anthropic-operated with same-day API parity — see `shared/claude-platform-on-aws.md` for client setup. For per-feature availability on **Claude Platform on AWS**, **Amazon Bedrock**, **Google Vertex AI**, and **Microsoft Foundry**, see `shared/platform-availability.md` — that table is the single source of truth in this skill; do not infer availability from anywhere else.

### Decision Tree

```
What does your application need?

0. Which provider?
   ├── First-party API or Claude Platform on AWS → continue (full surface available; per-feature exceptions in shared/platform-availability.md).
   └── Amazon Bedrock, Google Vertex AI, or Microsoft Foundry → Claude API (+ tool use for agents); see shared/platform-availability.md for per-feature support.

1. Single LLM call (classification, summarization, extraction, Q&A)
   └── Claude API — one request, one response

2. Do you want Anthropic to run the agent loop and host a per-session
   container where Claude executes tools (bash, file ops, code)?
   └── Yes → Managed Agents — server-managed sessions, persisted agent configs,
       SSE event stream, Skills + MCP, file mounts.
       Examples: "stateful coding agent with a workspace per task",
                 "long-running research agent that streams events to a UI",
                 "agent with persisted, versioned config used across many sessions"

3. Workflow (multi-step, code-orchestrated, with your own tools)
   └── Claude API with tool use — you control the loop

4. Open-ended agent (model decides its own trajectory, your own tools, you host the compute)
   └── Claude API agentic loop (maximum flexibility)
```

### Should I Build an Agent?

Before choosing the agent tier, check all four criteria:

- **Complexity** — Is the task multi-step and hard to fully specify in advance? (e.g., "turn this design doc into a PR" vs. "extract the title from this PDF")
- **Value** — Does the outcome justify higher cost and latency?
- **Viability** — Is Claude capable at this task type?
- **Cost of error** — Can errors be caught and recovered from? (tests, review, rollback)

If the answer is "no" to any of these, stay at a simpler tier (single call or workflow).

---

## Architecture

Everything goes through `POST /v1/messages`. Tools and output constraints are features of this single endpoint — not separate APIs.

**User-defined tools** — You define tools (via decorators, Zod schemas, or raw JSON), and the SDK's tool runner handles calling the API, executing your functions, and looping until Claude is done. For full control, you can write the loop manually.

**Server-side tools** — Anthropic-hosted tools that run on Anthropic's infrastructure. Code execution is fully server-side (declare it in `tools`, Claude runs code automatically). Computer use can be server-hosted or self-hosted.

**Structured outputs** — Constrains the Messages API response format (`output_config.format`) and/or tool parameter validation (`strict: true`). The recommended approach is `client.messages.parse()` which validates responses against your schema automatically. Note: the old `output_format` parameter is deprecated; use `output_config: {format: {...}}` on `messages.create()`.

**Supporting endpoints** — Batches (`POST /v1/messages/batches`), Files (`POST /v1/files`), Token Counting (`POST /v1/messages/count_tokens` — see `shared/token-counting.md`), and Models (`GET /v1/models`, `GET /v1/models/{id}` — live capability/context-window discovery) feed into or support Messages API requests.

---

## Current Models (cached: 2026-06-24)

| Model             | Model ID            | Context        | Input $/1M | Output $/1M |
| ----------------- | ------------------- | -------------- | ---------- | ----------- |
| Claude Fable 5    | `claude-fable-5`      | 1M             | $10.00     | $50.00      |
| Claude Mythos 5 (Project Glasswing only) | `claude-mythos-5` | 1M | $10.00     | $50.00      |
| Claude Opus 4.8   | `claude-opus-4-8`   | 1M             | $5.00      | $25.00      |
| Claude Opus 4.7   | `claude-opus-4-7`   | 1M             | $5.00      | $25.00      |
| Claude Opus 4.6   | `claude-opus-4-6`   | 1M             | $5.00      | $25.00      |
| Claude Sonnet 5   | `claude-sonnet-5`   | 1M             | $3.00 ($2.00 intro through 2026-08-31) | $15.00 ($10.00 intro) |
| Claude Sonnet 4.6 | `claude-sonnet-4-6` | 1M             | $3.00      | $15.00      |
| Claude Haiku 4.5  | `claude-haiku-4-5`  | 200K           | $1.00      | $5.00       |

**ALWAYS use `claude-opus-4-8` unless the user explicitly names a different model.** This is non-negotiable. Do not use `claude-sonnet-5`, `claude-sonnet-4-6`, or any other model unless the user literally says "use sonnet" or "use haiku". Never downgrade for cost — that's the user's decision, not yours. Use `claude-fable-5` only when the user explicitly asks for Claude Fable 5, "fable", or Anthropic's most capable model — it has different API behavior than the Opus family (see below) and pricing that exceeds Opus-tier.

### Claude Fable 5 (`claude-fable-5`) — most capable widely released model

Claude Fable 5 is Anthropic's most capable widely released model, for the most demanding reasoning and long-horizon agentic work. **Claude Mythos 5** (`claude-mythos-5`) offers the same capabilities, pricing, and API surface through Project Glasswing (participation is the only way to access it), succeeding the invitation-only Claude Mythos Preview (`claude-mythos-preview`) — everything below applies to both models. 1M context window (the maximum is also the default), 128K max output. Key API differences from Opus-tier — see `shared/model-migration.md` → Migrating to Claude Fable 5 for details:

- **Thinking is always on** — omit the `thinking` parameter entirely (or send `{type: "adaptive"}`). Any other explicit configuration is rejected: `{type: "disabled"}` and `{type: "enabled", budget_tokens: N}` both return a 400. Control depth with `output_config.effort` (supports `low` through `xhigh` and `max`).
- **The raw chain of thought is never returned** — responses carry regular `thinking` blocks (not `redacted_thinking`): `display: "summarized"` returns a readable summary, `"omitted"` (the default) leaves the `thinking` field as an empty string. Replay rules: pass thinking blocks back exactly as received on the same model (including empty-text blocks — the API rejects *modified* blocks, not read ones); a **different** model **drops** them from the prompt (typically silently — not an error; the drop happens before pricing, so dropped blocks aren't billed and there's nothing to strip). Regular thinking blocks from other models replay across models freely.
- **Tokenizer** — same tokenizer as Opus 4.8 (introduced with Opus 4.7). Token counts are roughly unchanged when migrating from Opus 4.7/4.8; per-token pricing differs. Coming from Opus 4.6, Sonnet, Haiku, or older, re-baseline with `count_tokens`.
- **`refusal` stop reason — handle it, and opt into fallbacks by default** — safety classifiers may decline a request (HTTP 200, `stop_reason: "refusal"`, with a `stop_details` category). A pre-output refusal has an empty `content` array and is not billed at all; a mid-stream refusal bills the already-streamed output — discard the partial output. Always check `stop_reason` before reading `content`. Recovery is **opt-in on the API**: most Claude consumer surfaces ship with built-in Claude Opus 4.8 fallbacks, but an API request that doesn't opt in simply stops on a refusal — and false positives on benign adjacent work (security tooling, life-sciences tasks) do happen. **When you write `claude-fable-5` code, include the server-side `fallbacks` parameter by default** (`betas: ["server-side-fallback-2026-06-01"]` + `fallbacks: [{"model": "claude-opus-4-8"}]`; Claude API and Claude Platform on AWS): a declined request is transparently re-served by the fallback model inside the same call, with credit-style repricing applied automatically (a decline before any output isn't billed; the rescue bills at the fallback model's own rates). Tell the user you've enabled it; drop it only if they decline. The GA SDKs' client-side `BetaRefusalFallbackMiddleware` + `BetaFallbackState` handle retry everywhere server-side fallbacks aren't supported (incl. Amazon Bedrock, Vertex AI, Microsoft Foundry); fallback credit refunds the cache-switch cost of client-side retries. Code examples: the Refusal Fallbacks section of your language's claude-api doc; full semantics in the migration guide's refusal section.
- **No assistant prefill** — same as the rest of the 4.6+ family.
- **30-day data retention required** — Claude Fable 5 is not available under zero data retention; requests from an org whose retention configuration doesn't meet the requirement return `400 invalid_request_error`.
- **Longer turns, different prompting** — single requests on hard tasks can run many minutes (plan timeouts/streaming/progress UX); effort sweeps should include low/medium for routine work; prompts written for prior models are often too prescriptive and reduce output quality. See `shared/model-migration.md` → Migrating to Claude Fable 5 → Behavioral shifts (prompt-tunable) for the recommended prompt snippets (anti-overplanning, no-tidying, grounded progress claims, boundaries, async sub-agents, memory, `send_to_user`).

**CRITICAL: Use only the exact model ID strings from the table above — they are complete as-is. Do not append date suffixes.** For example, use `claude-sonnet-4-6`, never `claude-sonnet-4-6-20251114` or any other date-suffixed variant you might recall from training data. If the user requests an older model not in the table (e.g., "opus 4.5", "sonnet 3.7"), read `shared/models.md` for the exact ID — do not construct one yourself.

A note: if any of the model strings above look unfamiliar to you, that's to be expected — that just means they were released after your training data cutoff. Rest assured they are real models; we wouldn't mess with you like that.

**Live capability lookup:** The table above is cached. When the user asks "what's the context window for X", "does X support vision/thinking/effort", or "which models support Y", query the Models API (`client.models.retrieve(id)` / `client.models.list()`) — see `shared/models.md` for the field reference and capability-filter examples.

---

## Authentication (Quick Reference)

**An unset `ANTHROPIC_API_KEY` does NOT mean there are no credentials.** The SDKs and the `ant` CLI resolve credentials in this order (first match wins): `ANTHROPIC_API_KEY` → `ANTHROPIC_AUTH_TOKEN` → the `ANTHROPIC_PROFILE`-selected or active OAuth profile from `ant auth login` → Workload Identity Federation env vars → the default profile on disk. A bare `Anthropic()` / `new Anthropic()` / `anthropic.NewClient()` works after `ant auth login` with no env var set.

**When you need to call the API and `ANTHROPIC_API_KEY` is unset, don't ask the user for a key.** First run `ant auth status` — it shows which credential source and profile is active. If it reports an active profile:

- **SDK code or `ant` CLI:** just run it. The zero-arg client constructor and every `ant …` subcommand pick up the profile automatically — no env var needed.
- **Raw `curl` / HTTP:** get a short-lived token with `ant auth print-credentials --access-token` and send it as `Authorization: Bearer <token>` **plus** the header `anthropic-beta: oauth-2025-04-20` (OAuth tokens go on `Authorization: Bearer`, not `x-api-key:` — converting a curl from an API key is a header change, not a key swap). Always pass `--access-token`; the no-flag form prints JSON, not a bare token.

Only ask the user for a key if `ant auth status` reports no active credential source (or `ant` itself isn't installed). Suggest `ant auth login` as the first option — it stores a profile under `~/.config/anthropic/` that the SDKs read automatically — and an exported `ANTHROPIC_API_KEY` as the alternative.

Full auth details (named profiles, scopes, the API-key-shadows-profile trap, refresh-token expiry): `shared/anthropic-cli.md`.

---

## Thinking & Effort (Quick Reference)

**Fable 5 / Opus 4.8 / 4.7 / Sonnet 5 — Adaptive thinking only:** Use `thinking: {type: "adaptive"}`. `thinking: {type: "enabled", budget_tokens: N}` returns a 400 — adaptive is the only on-mode. On Opus 4.8, Opus 4.7, and Sonnet 5, `{type: "disabled"}` and omitting `thinking` both work (on Sonnet 5, omitting runs adaptive; on Opus 4.7/4.8, omitting runs without thinking — set `{type: "adaptive"}` explicitly); on Fable 5, an explicit `{type: "disabled"}` returns a 400 — omit the `thinking` param entirely instead. Sampling parameters (`temperature`, `top_p`, `top_k`) are also removed and will 400. Opus 4.8 keeps the same request surface as 4.7 (no new breaking changes) — see `shared/model-migration.md` → Migrating to Opus 4.8 for the behavioral re-tuning, and → Migrating to Opus 4.7 for the full breaking-change list when coming from 4.6 or earlier. Note: with `thinking` disabled, Opus 4.8 may write longer reasoning into the visible response — leave adaptive thinking on, or add a final-answer-only instruction (see the migration guide).
**Opus 4.6 — Adaptive thinking (recommended):** Use `thinking: {type: "adaptive"}`. Claude dynamically decides when and how much to think. No `budget_tokens` needed — `budget_tokens` is deprecated on Opus 4.6 and Sonnet 4.6 and should not be used for new code. Adaptive thinking also automatically enables interleaved thinking (no beta header needed). **When the user asks for "extended thinking", a "thinking budget", or `budget_tokens`: always use Fable 5, Opus 4.8, 4.7, or 4.6 with `thinking: {type: "adaptive"}`. The concept of a fixed token budget for thinking is deprecated — adaptive thinking replaces it. Do NOT use `budget_tokens` for new 4.6/4.7/4.8 code and do NOT switch to an older model.** *Gradual-migration carve-out:* `budget_tokens` is still functional on Opus 4.6 and Sonnet 4.6 as a transitional escape hatch — if you're migrating existing code and need a hard token ceiling before you've tuned `effort`, see `shared/model-migration.md` → Transitional escape hatch. Note: this carve-out does **not** apply to Fable 5, Opus 4.7 or 4.8 — `budget_tokens` is fully removed there.
**Effort parameter (GA, no beta header):** Controls thinking depth and overall token spend via `output_config: {effort: "low"|"medium"|"high"|"max"}` (inside `output_config`, not top-level). Default is `high` (equivalent to omitting it). `max` is supported on Fable 5, Opus 4.6 and later, Sonnet 5, and Sonnet 4.6 (not Haiku or earlier Sonnets). Opus 4.7 added `"xhigh"` (between `high` and `max`) — the best setting for most coding and agentic use cases on Fable 5 / Opus 4.7/4.8 / Sonnet 5, and the default in Claude Code; use a minimum of `high` for most intelligence-sensitive work. Works on Fable 5, Opus 4.5, Opus 4.6, Opus 4.7, Opus 4.8, Sonnet 5, and Sonnet 4.6. Will error on Sonnet 4.5 / Haiku 4.5. On Fable 5, Opus 4.7/4.8, and Sonnet 5, effort matters more than on any prior model in their tier — re-tune it when migrating, and run long-horizon/agentic tasks at `high`/`xhigh` with the full task spec given up front. Combine with adaptive thinking for the best cost-quality tradeoffs. Lower effort means fewer and more-consolidated tool calls, less preamble, and terser confirmations — `high` is often the sweet spot balancing quality and token efficiency; use `max` when correctness matters more than cost; use `low` for subagents or simple tasks.

**Thinking display — `"omitted"` by default on Fable 5 / Mythos 5 / Opus 4.8 / 4.7 / Sonnet 5:** `display: "summarized"` returns a readable summary of the reasoning; `"omitted"` (the default on all five — a silent change from Opus 4.6 and Sonnet 4.6, where it was `"summarized"`) streams `thinking` blocks with empty text. `display` controls visibility only — thinking happens and is billed the same under every setting; the raw chain of thought is never exposed on any model. If you stream reasoning to users, the default looks like a long pause before output — set `thinking: {type: "adaptive", display: "summarized"}` explicitly. (Independent of display, echo thinking blocks back unchanged when continuing on the same model; other models silently ignore them — see the migration guide.)

**Task Budgets (beta, Fable 5 / Opus 4.7 / 4.8 / Sonnet 5):** `output_config: {task_budget: {type: "tokens", total: N}}` tells the model how many tokens it has for a full agentic loop — it sees a running countdown and self-moderates (minimum 20,000; beta header `task-budgets-2026-03-13`). Distinct from `max_tokens`, which is an enforced per-response ceiling the model is not aware of. See `shared/model-migration.md` → Task Budgets.

**Sonnet 4.6:** Supports adaptive thinking (`thinking: {type: "adaptive"}`). `budget_tokens` is deprecated on Sonnet 4.6 — use adaptive thinking instead.

**Older models (only if explicitly requested):** If the user specifically asks for Sonnet 4.5 or another older model, use `thinking: {type: "enabled", budget_tokens: N}`. `budget_tokens` must be less than `max_tokens` (minimum 1024). Never choose an older model just because the user mentions `budget_tokens` — use Opus 4.8 with adaptive thinking instead.

---

## Compaction (Quick Reference)

**Beta, Fable 5, Opus 4.8, Opus 4.7, Opus 4.6, Sonnet 5, and Sonnet 4.6.** For long-running conversations that may exceed the 1M context window, enable server-side compaction. The API automatically summarizes earlier context when it approaches the trigger threshold (default: 150K tokens). Requires beta header `compact-2026-01-12`.

**Critical:** Append `response.content` (not just the text) back to your messages on every turn. Compaction blocks in the response must be preserved — the API uses them to replace the compacted history on the next request. Extracting only the text string and appending that will silently lose the compaction state.

See `{lang}/claude-api/README.md` (Compaction section) for code examples. Full docs via WebFetch in `shared/live-sources.md`.

---

## Prompt Caching (Quick Reference)

**Prefix match.** Any byte change anywhere in the prefix invalidates everything after it. Render order is `tools` → `system` → `messages`. Keep stable content first (frozen system prompt, deterministic tool list), put volatile content (timestamps, per-request IDs, varying questions) after the last `cache_control` breakpoint.

**Mid-conversation operator instructions** (Claude Opus 4.8 only; no beta header): append `{"role": "system", ...}` to `messages[]` instead of editing top-level `system`. Preserves the cached history prefix and is the prompt-injection-safe operator channel. See `shared/prompt-caching.md` § Mid-conversation system messages.

**Top-level auto-caching** (`cache_control: {type: "ephemeral"}` on `messages.create()`) is the simplest option when you don't need fine-grained placement. Max 4 breakpoints per request. Minimum cacheable prefix is ~1024 tokens — shorter prefixes silently won't cache.

**Verify with `usage.cache_read_input_tokens`** — if it's zero across repeated requests, a silent invalidator is at work (`datetime.now()` in system prompt, unsorted JSON, varying tool set).

For placement patterns, architectural guidance, and the silent-invalidator audit checklist: read `shared/prompt-caching.md`. Language-specific syntax: `{lang}/claude-api/README.md` (Prompt Caching section).

---

## Fast Mode (Quick Reference)

**Research preview, Opus 4.8 / 4.7 only.** Opus 4.7 fast mode is deprecated — after removal, `speed: "fast"` on 4.7 returns an error. Opus 4.8 is the durable fast-capable tier. Fast mode runs the same model at up to 2.5x higher output tokens per second, at premium pricing. Three things are required on every request: use the **beta** messages endpoint (`client.beta.messages.…`), pass the beta flag `fast-mode-2026-02-01`, and set `speed: "fast"` as a top-level request parameter (not a header, not in `extra_body`).

```python
client.beta.messages.create(
    model="claude-opus-4-8", max_tokens=4096,
    speed="fast", betas=["fast-mode-2026-02-01"],
    messages=[...],
)
```

| Language | Beta flag | Speed parameter |
|---|---|---|
| Python | `betas=["fast-mode-2026-02-01"]` | `speed="fast"` |
| TypeScript / Ruby | `betas: ["fast-mode-2026-02-01"]` | `speed: "fast"` |
| Go | `[]anthropic.AnthropicBeta{anthropic.AnthropicBetaFastMode2026_02_01}` | `Speed: anthropic.BetaMessageNewParamsSpeedFast` |
| Java | `.addBeta(AnthropicBeta.FAST_MODE_2026_02_01)` | `.speed(MessageCreateParams.Speed.FAST)` |
| C# | `Betas = ["fast-mode-2026-02-01"]` | `Speed = Speed.Fast` (`Anthropic.Models.Beta.Messages`) |
| PHP | `betas: ['fast-mode-2026-02-01']` | `speed: 'fast'` |
| cURL | `anthropic-beta: fast-mode-2026-02-01` header | `"speed": "fast"` in body |

`response.usage.speed` reports which speed was used. Fast mode has its own rate limit separate from standard Opus; on 429, either retry after the `retry-after` delay or drop `speed` and fall back to standard (note: switching speed invalidates prompt cache). Not available with Batch API, Priority Tier, Claude Platform on AWS, or third-party platforms.

---

## Task Budgets (Quick Reference)

**Beta, Fable 5 / Sonnet 5 / Opus 4.8 / 4.7.** A task budget gives Claude a token ceiling for an agentic loop so it paces itself and finishes gracefully instead of being cut off. Set `task_budget` inside `output_config` on `client.beta.messages.stream(...)` with beta flag `task-budgets-2026-03-13` — use streaming so the large `max_tokens` doesn't hit HTTP timeouts:

```python
with client.beta.messages.stream(
    model="claude-opus-4-8", max_tokens=128000,
    output_config={"effort": "high", "task_budget": {"type": "tokens", "total": 64000}},
    betas=["task-budgets-2026-03-13"],
    messages=[...], tools=[...],
) as stream:
    response = stream.get_final_message()
```

`task_budget` fields: `type` (always `"tokens"`), `total`, and optional `remaining` (defaults to `total`). The server injects a countdown marker Claude sees during generation; the budget counts what Claude generates and the tool results it reads this turn — **not** the full history you resend each request.

**Observing spend:** accumulate `response.usage.output_tokens` (plus the token count of the tool-result blocks you append) across loop iterations if you want to display progress. Leave `remaining` unset in the normal loop — the server tracks the countdown itself, and passing a client-computed `remaining` while also resending full history under-reports the budget. **Only pass `remaining`** when you compact or rewrite history between requests and the server can no longer derive prior spend.

---

## Provider Clients (Quick Reference)

When targeting Claude on a third-party platform, use that platform's dedicated client class — not the first-party `Anthropic()` client with a `base_url` override. After construction the client exposes the same `messages.create` / `.stream` surface as the first-party SDK.

### Amazon Bedrock

Use the **Mantle** client (Messages-API Bedrock endpoint). Bedrock model IDs take an `anthropic.` prefix (e.g. `"anthropic.claude-opus-4-8"`). Region is required.

| Language | Client |
|---|---|
| Python | `from anthropic import AnthropicBedrockMantle` → `AnthropicBedrockMantle(aws_region="…")` |
| TypeScript | `import { AnthropicBedrockMantle } from "@anthropic-ai/bedrock-sdk"` → `new AnthropicBedrockMantle({ awsRegion: "…" })` |
| Go | `bedrock.NewMantleClient(ctx, bedrock.MantleClientConfig{ AWSRegion: "…" })` |
| Java | `AnthropicOkHttpClient.builder().backend(BedrockMantleBackend.fromEnv()).build()` (from `com.anthropic.bedrock.backends`) |
| C# | `new AnthropicBedrockMantleClient(new() { AwsRegion = "…" })` (package `Anthropic.Bedrock`) |
| PHP | `use Anthropic\Bedrock\MantleClient;` → `new MantleClient(awsRegion: '…')` |
| Ruby | `Anthropic::BedrockMantleClient.new(aws_region: "…")` |

`AnthropicBedrock` / `BedrockClient` / `BedrockBackend` (without `Mantle`) are the legacy `bedrock-runtime` InvokeModel path — prefer the Mantle client for new code.

### Microsoft Foundry

| Language | Client |
|---|---|
| Python | `from anthropic import AnthropicFoundry` → `AnthropicFoundry(api_key=…, resource="…")` |
| TypeScript | `import AnthropicFoundry from "@anthropic-ai/foundry-sdk"` → `new AnthropicFoundry({ … })` |
| Java | `AnthropicOkHttpClient.builder().backend(FoundryBackend.fromEnv()).build()` (from `com.anthropic.foundry.backends`) |
| C# | `new AnthropicFoundryClient(new AnthropicFoundryApiKeyCredentials(…))` (package `Anthropic.Foundry`) |
| PHP | `Foundry\Client::withCredentials(…)` |
